retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Nevertheless, the absence of physical findings cannot exclude the diagnosis of endometriosis. The gold standard diagnostic tool remains laparoscopy, combined with an abdominal cavity exploration and a histological biopsy. [30] The lesions can differ in size and color. They can appear as red, white, or clear vesicular. Black "powder burns" or "gunshot" lesions are brown or black endometriotic tissue. [31] The role of histological confirmation is a bit controversial since macroscopically detected endometriotic lesions cannot always be verified histologically, and vice versa, namely in a macroscopically normal peritoneum, can be detected endometriosis tissue. [30] Direct visualization of the endometriotic lesion alone without a histological confirmation lacks enough diagnostic value since, to a great extent, it relies on the surgical skills and capability of the physician. Because diagnostic laparoscopy is an invasive procedure, it entails risks associated with any invasive process and could lead, albeit rare, to severe complications. Hence, there is a need for a diagnostic transition from surgical to non-surgical options, which could also contribute to a decrease in the time between the onset of symptoms and the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Female Infertility -- Pathophysiology -- Endometriosis
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is defined as endometrial tissue outside the uterine cavity. The diagnosis is based on the histological identification of endometrial glands or stroma outside the uterus. Endometriosis is most commonly found in the pelvis but can spread throughout the entire abdomen and affects 10% to 15% of reproductive-age women. [19] Of women with endometriosis, 40% to 50% experience infertility. [20] Endometriosis is categorized into 4 stages, according to the American Society of Reproductive Medicine, with stage I being minimal and stage IV severe. Endometriosis is known to cause infertility, but the pathophysiology is thought to change according to the stage. [21] For stages I and II, infertility is believed to be associated with inflammation and increased production of prostaglandins and cytokines, macrophages, and natural killer cells. [22] The inflammation impairs ovarian and tubal function, resulting in defective follicular formation, fertilization, and implantation. [23] Stages III and IV are associated with pelvic adhesions or masses that distort pelvic anatomy; this inherently impairs tubal motility, oocyte release, and sperm motility. [24] Also, advanced endometriosis is hypothesized to impair folliculogenesis, reducing fertilization potential. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The role of steroid hormones in the pathogenesis of endometriosis is also indisputable. Estrogen is the main hormone responsible for the propagation and expansion of the endometrium. The increased action of aromatase, mainly in the deep infiltrating endometriosis, leads to locally increased estrogen activity. [10] On the other hand, the inability of progesterone to have an antagonistic action with estrogen in the endometrial tissue is a determining factor for establishing endometriosis. [12] The epigenetic changes are also an undeniable factor in the pathogenesis of endometriosis, and this is demonstrated by the fact that not only women of reproductive age suffer from the disease but also adolescents and younger women with a family history. [13] Specific genetic loci have also been identified for the initiation of the disease, making some women more prone to the disease than others. However, the genetic profile is not yet fully understood. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrioma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The gold standard for the diagnosis of endometriosis is via laparoscopy. Because imaging and laboratory studies are of limited benefit in diagnosing endometriosis, direct visualization through surgery is the standard. During laparoscopy, endometriosis lesions will typically appear blue or black. However, they can appear as red, white, or non-pigmented lesions. At this time, the severity of the disease can also be evaluated. If there are significant adhesions, peritoneal defects, or endometriomas present, this is indicative of a more severe disease. The visualized lesions can be biopsied and evaluated by pathology for endometrial glands and stroma. [36] If the patient is also experiencing infertility issues, chromotubation can also be completed at this time to assess tubal patency.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrioma -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The only way to confirm the diagnosis of endometriosis, including endometriomas, is to surgically diagnose it using direct visualization and tissue samples. The biopsy must contain both endometrial glands and stroma to confirm the presence of endometriosis in the tissue. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The endometriotic lesions in ovaries are described as ovarian endometriomas or as pseudocysts. The frequency of ovarian endometriomas accounts for approximately 17% to 44% of women with endometriosis, are bilateral in 50% of the cases [24] , and are twice more frequent in the left than in the right ovary due to anatomic variables. [25] They differentiate from the common ovarian cysts by the fact that the endometriotic tissue bleeds inside of the endometriotic cyst, is a well-defined extra ovarian hematoma, and is surrounded by a duplicated ovarian parenchyma, lined by endometrial stroma, epithelium, and glands. [7] Chocolate fluid accumulates, covering the wall and filling in the cyst, containing old degenerated blood products, like hemosiderin-filled macrophages and pigmented histiocytes, resulting in a chocolate-like appearance in laparoscopic imaging. [24] Deep infiltrating endometriosis refers to endometriotic lesions that infiltrate the peritoneum more than 5 mm and cause severe symptomatology. [26] Deep endometriosis can involve additionally the bladder, the ureter, and the bowel, more frequently the rectovaginal septum, and less commonly the sigmoid. [26] Superficial peritoneal lesions are, in most cases, an accidental finding. Still, they sporadically can cause a thickening or hemorrhage of the mucosa or even be the reason for a pathological cervicovaginal smear. The histologic diagnosis of endometriosis could be hindered or obscured by a non-typical appearance of the endometrial glands or even the stroma caused by inflammation, edema, or hemorrhage. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Endometriosis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Core needle biopsy findings frequently include nonciliated columnar epithelium-lined tubular glands, hemosiderin-laden macrophages, and a fibrotic stroma. The definitive diagnostic method remains the histopathologic examination of excised tissue, which often displays glandular structures surrounded by stromal cells, dense fibrous scarring, and hemosiderin deposits within fibroconnective tissue and subcutaneous layers. [13] [9] [6] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrioma -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: As can be seen by these descriptions, if an endometrioma is present, the patient is already a stage 3 or 4. Most patients are found to have stage 1 or 2 endometriosis. However, staging does not always coincide with the severity of symptoms. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, some women suffering from endometriosis experience allodynia, a phenomenon when, even with the application of a nonpainful stimulus, an intolerable painful reaction is released. This condition indicates neuropathic pain. In the case of deep infiltrating endometriosis, the neural damage is caused by the invasion of endometrial stromal cells and mediators such as serotonin, histamine, prostaglandins, and nerve growth factor. These agents are released from mast cells, activated macrophages, and leukocytes, directly damaging the sensory nerve fibers and circulating in the peritoneal fluid. [7] Three subtypes of endometriosis often overlap with each other. These are the superficial peritoneal lesions, the ovarian endometrioma, and the deep infiltrating endometriosis. [20] Notably, the degree of clinical manifestations of the patient is not directly associated with the extent of the disease or the size of endometriosis lesions. [21] Patients with bowel endometriosis often present with a wide range of gastrointestinal symptoms like diarrhea, constipation, abdominal pain, or bloating and could mimic other clinical conditions like inflammatory bowel disease or irritable bowel syndrome. [22] Rectovaginal endometriosis is a very severe deep infiltrating form of the disease involving the vagina, the rectum, and the rectovaginal septum. Most often, it can be presented with bowel irritation, dyspareunia, dysmenorrhea, dyschezia, and rectal bleeding coinciding with menstrual bleeding. [23] Finally, a symptom with great concern is infertility, which often leads clinicians to suspect endometriosis, even in asymptomatic patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The disease diagnosis is usually delayed an average of 4 to 11 years from the onset of symptoms. This phenomenon occurs in low- and middle-income countries and wealthy societies with universal healthcare access. [19] This delay is attributed to the non-existence of a pathognomonic test or biomarker to detect the disease but also to the diversity of symptoms that could be considered physiologic responses during menstruation (like pain and discomfort), and the wide range of reported symptoms overlap with other gastrointestinal or gynecological causes. To diagnose endometriosis properly, the physician should start by taking a detailed history and performing a gynecological physical examination. Positive family history, pelvic pain, benign ovarian cysts, pelvic surgeries, and infertility issues enhance and alarm physicians to diagnose endometriosis. [19] Physical examination reveals variable findings, depending on the location and size of the endometriotic lesion. [28] Tenderness on vaginal examination, palpable nodules in the posterior fornix, adnexal masses, and immobility of the uterus are diagnostically indicating findings of endometriosis. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrioma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriomas are the most common manifestation of endometriosis on the ovary. However, implants can also be found throughout the abdomen, such as on the bowel, within prior surgical incisions, and even in rare cases in distant locations of the body, such as the cerebellum. [2] Approximately 17 to 44% of women diagnosed with endometriosis will experience an endometrioma. These lesions are commonly referred to as chocolate cysts due to the thick dark brown appearance of the fluid contained within them. [3] Endometriomas indicate a more severe disease state in patients with endometriosis and can lead to specific issues, such as decreased ovarian reserve. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the risk factors for developing endometriosis and summarize the epidemiology of the disease. Evaluate the history and physical exam findings associated with endometriosis and describe the available ways to evaluate the disease. Differentiate the treatment and management options available for endometriosis. Communicate the importance of interprofessional team collaboration to improve the outcomes of endometriosis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Uterus -- Pathophysiology -- Endometriosis
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis occurs when endometrial tissues migrate outside of the endometrial cavity and implant in another part of the body. Once improperly implanted, endometrial cells start to grow and form a mass of endometrial tissue. Endometriosis most often affects the bilateral ovaries, and forms a blood-filled cyst, called a chocolate cyst. However, endometriosis can affect any structure in the abdomen and pelvis, including the fallopian tubes, uterine ligaments, rectouterine pouch (pouch of Douglas), the bladder, and the intestines. These endometrial tissue implants appear as a superficial yellow-brown 'gun-powder' lesion. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrioma -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cytokines and prostaglandins are signaling molecules for creating an inflammatory response and thus generate inflammation in the area of the endometriotic implantation. This inflammatory response then lays the foundation for the production of new vascularization and new fibrous tissue formation. This snowball effect then creates the adhesions and pain commonly associated with this disease process. These issues also lead to the main complications of this disease, such as infertility and chronic pelvic pain. Patients with endometriomas have a more severe disease state and typically experience this on a more significant scale than those with stage one or two endometriosis. [9] [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Endometriosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a gynecological condition characterized by the presence of endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus, typically at sites including the ovary, uterosacral ligaments, ovarian fossa, the pouch of Douglas, and the bladder, thought to be precipitated by genetic and epigenetic mutations that can predispose individuals to the disease, leading to infertility and immune system alterations. [1] [2] Contrary to the long-held belief that endometriosis is a progressive and recurrent disease, research indicates that most lesions are self-limiting due to fibrosis and immune response. Emerging research emphasizes the role of immune dysfunction, hormonal influences, and environmental factors in disease onset and progression. The condition presents with diverse symptoms, including pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, but its progression and severity vary among individuals. Diagnosis remains challenging, often leading to delayed recognition, with surgical assessment via laparoscopy historically considered the gold standard. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Endometriosis ," for further information.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrioma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a common, estrogen-dependent, inflammatory, gynecologic disease process in which normal endometrial tissue is abnormally present outside the uterine cavity. Endometriomas are cystic lesions that stem from endometriosis. Endometriomas are most commonly found in the ovaries. Endometriosis affects approximately 10% of reproductive-aged women and is a common cause of chronic pain, dyspareunia, dysmenorrhea, and infertility. Most commonly, endometriosis is found within the pelvis, specifically on the ovaries. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2764
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a gynecological disorder characterized by the presence of endometrial‑like tissue that is normally located in the uterus, and its ectopic growth can cause pain, inflammation, and fertility issues. Understanding that endometriosis originates from tissue associated with the uterus is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2855
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is connected to endometriosis through a broader relationship, indicating that endometriosis falls within the scope of disorders affecting the female reproductive system. Both Female Reproductive System Disorder and endometriosis are classified under the parent category اضطراب رحمي, which translates to “uterine disorder.” In this hierarchy, اضطراب رحمي serves as the overarching class, with Female Reproductive System Disorder representing a broader group of conditions and endometriosis representing a specific type of uterine disorder. This structure highlights how endometriosis is a particular manifestation of uterine pathology within the larger context of female reproductive system disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 537
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is linked hierarchically to both اضطراب الحمل (pregnancy disorder) and اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder) as parent categories, indicating that disorders of the female reproductive system can be considered subtypes of broader pregnancy‑related and uterine conditions. Within this overarching category, specific diseases such as cancer of the uterus and endometriosis are included. Endometriosis is further classified as a type of اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث (female genital organ disorder) and also as a form of اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder), reflecting its position both within the general female genital organ disorder hierarchy and the more specific uterine disorder group. The hierarchical structure therefore connects pregnancy disorders, uterine disorders, and female genital organ disorders, with Female Reproductive System Disorder, uterine cancer, and endometriosis positioned as specific manifestations within this network. This integrated view aids clinicians in understanding how various female reproductive conditions relate to each other and to broader reproductive health categories, supporting more comprehensive diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2635
retrieved_docs.content: Manual conversion of position is a medical procedure whose site is the uterus, indicating that it is performed on or involves the uterine organ. Endometriosis is a disease that is located in the uterus, reflecting the presence of ectopic endometrial tissue within or on the uterine structure. Both concepts highlight the uterus as a central focus for therapeutic interventions and pathological conditions, underscoring the importance of uterine health in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2877
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is classified as a type of uterine disorder (اضطراب رحمي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1455
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a medical condition that falls within the broader category of اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث (disorder of the female reproductive organs). This broader category is itself a subcategory of اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder), indicating that uterine disorders serve as the parent classification for disorders of the female genital organs. Accordingly, endometriosis is also classified directly as an اضطراب رحمي, reflecting its nature as a specific type of uterine disorder. Understanding these hierarchical relationships helps clinicians recognize that endometriosis is both a uterine disorder and part of the wider spectrum of female reproductive organ disorders, which is important for accurate diagnosis, management, and patient education.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2815
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is a broad category encompassing various gynecologic conditions. It includes specific disorders such as endometriosis, which is classified as a type of uterine disorder (اضطراب رحمي). The category also relates to the parent condition اضطراب رحمي itself and to ovarian cyst (كيسة مبيضية). Recognizing that endometriosis falls under the umbrella of uterine disorders within the larger Female Reproductive System Disorder framework helps clinicians assess and manage these interconnected conditions more comprehensively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 827
retrieved_docs.content: ulcers. The framework links female infertility of cervical origin to اضطراب في عنق الرحم and to العقم لدى الإناث, and associates female infertility with endometrial implantation, reflecting the contribution of endometriosis to reproductive difficulty. Diabetic neuropathy is highlighted as a key risk factor for lower‑extremity ulcers, underscoring the need for vigilant monitoring in diabetic patients. Overall, the integrated classification supports comprehensive documentation, risk assessment, and coordinated management across these interconnected medical entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2531
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is positioned within a hierarchical framework that includes both اضطراب الحمل (pregnancy disorder) and اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder) as its parent categories. اضطراب رحمي itself is a subcategory of اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث (female genital organ disorder), establishing a broader classification that encompasses disorders of the female reproductive tract. Endometriosis is specifically linked to اضطراب رحمي through an inverse‑isa relationship, indicating that endometriosis is a type of uterine disorder, and it also has a broader relationship with اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث, situating it within the wider group of female genital organ disorders. Consequently, the knowledge structure reflects a nested taxonomy: اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث → اضطراب رحمي → Endometriosis, while Female Reproductive System Disorder connects to both pregnancy‑related and uterine‑related disorders, highlighting the interrelated nature of these conditions for comprehensive clinical understanding and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1790
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are situated within the uterus and are linked to اضطراب رحمي, an Arabic term for uterine disorder. Endometriosis is likewise located in the uterus and is classified as a type of اضطراب رحمي, indicating that it is a specific uterine disorder. The shared uterine location and the common association with uterine disorders underscore the importance of recognizing that many pregnancy‑related complications and conditions such as endometriosis involve the uterus and fall under the broader category of uterine pathology, which is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2427
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory diseases of the female pelvic organs are identified as having the ovary (ovarium) as their finding site, indicating that the ovary is the primary location where such pelvic inflammatory conditions manifest. Likewise, any ovary problem is described as having its finding site in the ovary itself, reinforcing the ovary’s central role in the pathology of disorders that originate in this organ. In addition, the ovary (ovarium) is noted to be the anatomical location for an ovarian pregnancy (حمل مَبيضِيّ), highlighting that ectopic implantation of a gestational sac can occur within the ovary. Together, these relationships emphasize the ovary’s significance as both the site of inflammatory pelvic disease, the focal point for ovary‑specific problems, and a possible location for an ovarian pregnancy, all of which are important considerations in diagnosis and management of female pelvic health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 192
retrieved_docs.content: organization from the whole organ to its functional subunits. The ovarian follicle serves as a finding site for “ovarian cysts”, and the cyst itself is located within the ovarian follicle, emphasizing that cystic lesions commonly arise in or are detected in follicular structures. The ovarian cyst is classified as “كيسة مبيضية حميدة”, confirming its typical benign nature and guiding its clinical management. Finally, the ovary is recorded as the location for an “ovarian pregnancy” (“حمل مَبيضِيّ”), a rare ectopic gestation that occurs within ovarian tissue. Collectively, these relationships portray the ovary as a nexus connecting non‑inflammatory and inflammatory disorders, developmental anatomy, surgical considerations, cystic pathology, and unique clinical entities across multiple languages and classification systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 376
retrieved_docs.content: Reproductive System Disorder. Endometriosis exhibits a broader relationship with اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث and is linked to اضطراب رحمي through an inverse_isa relationship, indicating that uterine disorder is the more general class encompassing endometriosis. Overall, these relationships depict a multi‑layered network in which sexual function disorders, obstetric complications, and gynecologic conditions intersect. Recognizing that Female Reproductive System Disorder resides at the intersection of خلل الوظيفة الجنسية, pregnancy disorders such as اضطراب الحمل and مخاض قبل الأوان, and uterine pathology provides clinicians with a comprehensive framework for diagnosis, interdisciplinary management, and patient counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 908
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs, lesions of the ovary, and noninflammatory disorders of the ovary all share the ovary (ovarium) as their finding site, indicating that the ovary is the primary location for these diverse pathologic processes. In addition, the ovary can serve as the anatomical site of an ovarian pregnancy (حمل مَبيضِيّ), an ectopic gestation that occurs within the ovarian tissue. Together, these relationships underscore the central role of the ovary in both inflammatory and non‑inflammatory pelvic conditions as well as in rare pregnancy complications, highlighting the need for careful evaluation of ovarian involvement in female pelvic health and disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1865
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is situated within the broader category of uterine disorder (اضطراب رحمي). It serves as an overarching classification that includes specific gynecologic conditions such as cancer of uterus and endometriosis. Endometriosis is also directly classified as a type of uterine disorder, reinforcing its placement under the same parent category. This hierarchical organization highlights that uterine disorder (اضطراب رحمي) is the parent entity for both the general Female Reproductive System Disorder and the specific condition of endometriosis, while Female Reproductive System Disorder further encompasses uterine cancer as one of its constituent disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1091
retrieved_docs.content: encompasses organ‑specific biopsies (ovarian and uterine), the uterine biopsy further subdivides into endometrial biopsy, and all are situated within the broader histopathology framework overseen by obstetrics‑gynecology specialists, who also perform related procedures such as salpingo‑oophorectomy and endometrial sampling. This integrated structure aids clinicians in understanding procedural categorization, appropriate specialist involvement, and the interconnections among related diagnostic and therapeutic interventions.
